full_context: According to ISO 527-2012 "Plastics — Determination of tensile properties", Chapter 6, tensile stress is calculated by Formula (6.1): σ = F/A₀. Where: σ is tensile stress, MPa; F is applied force, N; A₀ is original cross‑sectional area of the specimen, mm². This formula is used for tensile property testing of materials.
potential_questions: Given a plastic specimen with original area A₀=50 mm² and applied force F=2500 N, calculate tensile stress σ.
task_description: 1. Obtain F and A₀; 2. Calculate σ = F/A₀; 3. Output σ.

full_context: According to ISO 6892-2016 "Metallic materials — Tensile testing", Appendix B, engineering strain is calculated by Formula (B.1): ε = (L − L₀)/L₀. Where: ε is engineering strain; L is gauge length after extension, mm; L₀ is original gauge length, mm. This formula is used to calculate material deformation.

variable_definitions.name: Engineering Strain
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of elongation to original length

variable_definitions.name: Length After Extension
variable_definitions.meaning: Gauge length of specimen after stretching

potential_questions: Given a metal specimen with original gauge L₀=50 mm and extended length L=62.5 mm, calculate engineering strain ε.
task_description: 1. Obtain L₀ and L; 2. Calculate L − L₀; 3. Calculate ε = (L − L₀)/L₀; 4. Output ε.

full_context: According to ASME B31.3-2018 "Process Piping", Chapter 302, hoop stress in a thin‑walled cylinder is calculated by Formula (302.1): S = PD/(2t). Where: S is hoop stress, MPa; P is internal pressure, MPa; D is internal pipe diameter, mm; t is wall thickness, mm. Used for pressure piping design.

variable_definitions.name: Hoop Stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Circumferential tensile stress under internal pressure

potential_questions: Given pipe ID D=200 mm, wall thickness t=10 mm, internal pressure P=2 MPa, calculate hoop stress S.
task_description: 1. Obtain P, D, t; 2. Calculate S = PD/(2t); 3. Output S.

full_context: According to API 650-2018 "Welded Tanks for Oil Storage", Chapter 5, design shell thickness is calculated by Formula (5.1): t = 4.9D(H−0.3)G/S_d. Where: t is design thickness, mm; D is tank internal diameter, m; H is design liquid level, m; G is specific gravity of stored liquid; S_d is allowable design stress, MPa. Used for tank wall thickness calculation.

variable_definitions.name: Design Thickness
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum required thickness of tank shell

variable_definitions.name: Liquid Specific Gravity
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of liquid density to water density

variable_definitions.name: Allowable Design Stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum permissible stress for the material

potential_questions: Given tank ID D=30 m, liquid level H=15 m, specific gravity G=0.85, allowable stress S_d=160 MPa, calculate design thickness t.
task_description: 1. Obtain D, H, G, S_d; 2. Calculate H−0.3; 3. Calculate 4.9 × D × (H−0.3) × G; 4. Calculate t = 4.9D(H−0.3)G/S_d; 5. Output t.

full_context: According to ISO 5167-2003 "Measurement of fluid flow by means of pressure differential devices", Chapter 5, flow rate through an orifice plate is calculated by Formula (5.1): Q = C_d A √[2ΔP/(ρ(1−β⁴))]. Where: Q is volumetric flow rate, m³/s; C_d is discharge coefficient; A is orifice bore area, m²; ΔP is differential pressure, Pa; ρ is fluid density, kg/m³; β is diameter ratio (d/D). Used for flow measurement.

variable_definitions.name: Volumetric Flow Rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Volume of fluid passing per unit time

variable_definitions.name: Discharge Coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient accounting for real flow effects

variable_definitions.name: Orifice Bore Area
variable_definitions.meaning: Cross‑sectional area of the orifice opening

variable_definitions.name: Differential Pressure
variable_definitions.meaning: Pressure difference across the orifice plate

variable_definitions.name: Diameter Ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of orifice bore to pipe ID

potential_questions: Given C_d=0.61, A=0.001 m², ΔP=10000 Pa, ρ=1000 kg/m³, β=0.5, calculate flow rate Q.
task_description: 1. Obtain C_d, A, ΔP, ρ, β; 2. Calculate β⁴; 3. Calculate 1−β⁴; 4. Calculate 2ΔP/(ρ(1−β⁴)); 5. Calculate √(2ΔP/(ρ(1−β⁴))); 6. Calculate Q = C_d × A × √(2ΔP/(ρ(1−β⁴))); 7. Output Q.

full_context: According to ISO 1219-2012 "Fluid power systems and components — Graphic symbols and circuit diagrams", Chapter 4, hydraulic power is calculated by Formula (4.1): P = pQ. Where: P is hydraulic power, W; p is pressure, Pa; Q is flow rate, m³/s. Used for energy calculation in hydraulic systems.

variable_definitions.name: Pressure
variable_definitions.meaning: Operating pressure of the hydraulic system

potential_questions: Given operating pressure p=10 MPa and flow rate Q=0.0001 m³/s, calculate hydraulic power P.
task_description: 1. Obtain p and Q; 2. Convert p to Pa; 3. Calculate P = p × Q; 4. Output P.

full_context: According to ISO 281-2007 "Rolling bearings — Dynamic load ratings and rating life", Appendix A, basic rating life is calculated by Formula (A.1): L₁₀ = (C/P)ᵖ. Where: L₁₀ is basic rating life, millions of revolutions; C is basic dynamic load rating, N; P is equivalent dynamic load, N; p is life exponent (p=3 for ball bearings, p=10/3 for roller bearings). Used for bearing life prediction.

variable_definitions.name: Basic Rating Life
variable_definitions.meaning: Life that 90% of bearings can achieve or exceed

variable_definitions.name: Basic Dynamic Load Rating
variable_definitions.meaning: Constant load that the bearing can sustain

variable_definitions.name: Equivalent Dynamic Load
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent constant load representing actual loading

potential_questions: Given ball bearing C=25 kN, equivalent load P=5 kN, p=3, calculate basic rating life L₁₀.
task_description: 1. Obtain C, P, p; 2. Calculate C/P; 3. Calculate L₁₀ = (C/P)ᵖ; 4. Output L₁₀.

full_context: According to ISO 6336-2006 "Calculation of load capacity of spur and helical gears", Chapter 5, contact stress on gear tooth surface is calculated by Formula (5.1): σ_H = Z_H Z_E Z_ε √[F_t/(b d₁) × (u+1)/u]. Where: σ_H is calculated contact stress, MPa; Z_H is zone factor; Z_E is elasticity coefficient; Z_ε is load sharing factor; F_t is tangential load, N; b is face width, mm; d₁ is pinion pitch diameter, mm; u is gear ratio. Used for gear strength verification.

variable_definitions.name: Contact Stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum stress at gear tooth contact area

variable_definitions.name: Zone Factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient for curvature at pitch point

variable_definitions.name: Pinion Pitch Diameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Pitch circle diameter of the pinion

variable_definitions.name: Gear Ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of gear teeth to pinion teeth

potential_questions: Given Z_H=2.5, Z_E=189.8 √MPa, Z_ε=0.9, F_t=2000 N, b=30 mm, d₁=100 mm, u=3, calculate contact stress σ_H.
task_description: 1. Obtain all parameters; 2. Calculate F_t/(b d₁); 3. Calculate (u+1)/u; 4. Calculate [F_t/(b d₁)] × [(u+1)/u]; 5. Calculate √(result from step 4); 6. Calculate σ_H = Z_H × Z_E × Z_ε × √(result from step 4); 7. Output σ_H.

full_context: According to ISO 1940-2003 "Mechanical vibration — Balance quality requirements for rotors in a constant (rigid) state", Chapter 4, unbalance is calculated by Formula (4.1): U = m e ω. Where: U is unbalance, g·mm; m is rotor mass, kg; e is eccentricity, μm; ω is angular velocity, rad/s. Used for rotor balancing calculation.

variable_definitions.name: Eccentricity
variable_definitions.meaning: Distance between mass center and rotation axis

potential_questions: Given rotor mass m=50 kg, eccentricity e=20 μm, speed n=3000 r/min, calculate unbalance U.
task_description: 1. Obtain m, e, n; 2. Calculate ω = 2πn/60; 3. Ensure consistent units; 4. Calculate U = m × e × ω; 5. Output U.

full_context: According to ISO 13709-2009 "Centrifugal pumps", Chapter 6, net positive suction head available is calculated by Formula (6.1): NPSH_a = (p₁ − p_v)/(ρg) + v₁²/(2g). Where: NPSH_a is available NPSH, m; p₁ is pump inlet pressure, Pa; p_v is saturation vapor pressure at fluid temperature, Pa; ρ is liquid density, kg/m³; g is gravitational acceleration, m/s²; v₁ is inlet velocity, m/s. Used to prevent pump cavitation.

variable_definitions.name: Net Positive Suction Head Available
variable_definitions.meaning: Excess energy above vaporization at pump inlet

variable_definitions.name: Saturation Vapor Pressure
variable_definitions.meaning: Vapor pressure of liquid at operating temperature

potential_questions: Given p₁=101325 Pa, p_v=2338 Pa, ρ=998 kg/m³, v₁=2 m/s, g=9.81 m/s², calculate NPSH_a.
task_description: 1. Obtain p₁, p_v, ρ, g, v₁; 2. Calculate p₁ − p_v; 3. Calculate (p₁ − p_v)/(ρg); 4. Calculate v₁²/(2g); 5. Calculate NPSH_a = result3 + result4; 6. Output NPSH_a.

full_context: According to ISO 14692-2017 "Petroleum and natural gas industries — Glass‑reinforced plastic pipes", Chapter 5, thermal stress is calculated by Formula (5.1): σ = E α ΔT. Where: σ is thermal stress, MPa; E is modulus of elasticity, MPa; α is linear expansion coefficient, 1/°C; ΔT is temperature change, °C. Used for thermal expansion analysis of pipes.

variable_definitions.name: Thermal Stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Stress due to constrained thermal expansion/contraction

variable_definitions.name: Temperature Change
variable_definitions.meaning: Difference between operating and installation temperature

potential_questions: Given E=25000 MPa, α=2×10⁻⁵ 1/°C, installation temp=20 °C, operating temp=80 °C, calculate thermal stress σ.
task_description: 1. Obtain E, α, ΔT; 2. Calculate ΔT = operating − installation temperature; 3. Calculate σ = E × α × ΔT; 4. Output σ.

full_context: According to ISO 2531-2009 "Ductile iron pipes, fittings and accessories for water or gas applications", Appendix B, thermal expansion is calculated by Formula (B.1): ΔL = α L₀ ΔT. Where: ΔL is length change, mm; α is linear expansion coefficient, 1/°C; L₀ is original pipe length, m; ΔT is temperature change, °C. Used for thermal compensation design of pipelines.

variable_definitions.name: Length Change
variable_definitions.meaning: Elongation or contraction due to temperature

potential_questions: Given α=1.2×10⁻⁵ 1/°C, L₀=100 m, installation=10 °C, operating=60 °C, calculate length change ΔL.
task_description: 1. Obtain α, L₀, ΔT; 2. Calculate ΔT = operating − installation temperature; 3. Convert L₀ to mm; 4. Calculate ΔL = α × L₀ × ΔT; 5. Output ΔL.

full_context: According to ISO 10816-2014 "Mechanical vibration — Evaluation of machine vibration by measurements on non‑rotating parts", Chapter 3, RMS vibration velocity is defined by Formula (3.1): v_rms = √[(1/T) ∫₀^T v²(t) dt]. Where: v_rms is root‑mean‑square vibration velocity, mm/s; T is measurement duration, s; v(t) is instantaneous vibration velocity, mm/s. Used for machine vibration assessment.
potential_questions: Given sinusoidal velocity v(t)=10 sin(100πt) mm/s, find RMS velocity v_rms.
task_description: 1. Identify sine form v(t)=Vₘ sin(ωt); 2. RMS = Vₘ/√2; 3. Substitute Vₘ=10 mm/s; 4. Calculate v_rms = 10/√2; 5. Output v_rms.

full_context: According to ISO 3744-2010 "Acoustics — Determination of sound power levels of noise sources using sound pressure — Engineering method in an essentially free field over a reflecting plane", Appendix C, sound pressure level is related to sound power level by Formula (C.1): L_p = L_W + 10 log₁₀[Q/(4πr²)]. Where: L_p is sound pressure level, dB; L_W is sound power level, dB; Q is directivity factor; r is measurement distance, m. Used for noise source power measurement.

variable_definitions.name: Sound Pressure Level
variable_definitions.meaning: 20 log₁₀ of ratio to reference sound pressure

variable_definitions.name: Sound Power Level
variable_definitions.meaning: 10 log₁₀ of ratio to reference sound power

variable_definitions.name: Measurement Distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Distance from microphone to source center

potential_questions: Given L_W=85 dB, Q=2, r=1 m, calculate sound pressure level L_p.
task_description: 1. Obtain L_W, Q, r; 2. Calculate 4πr²; 3. Calculate Q/(4πr²); 4. Calculate 10 log₁₀(Q/(4πr²)); 5. Calculate L_p = L_W + 10 log₁₀(Q/(4πr²)); 6. Output L_p.

full_context: According to ISO 12100-2010 "Safety of machinery — General principles for design — Risk assessment and risk reduction", Chapter 5, safety distance is calculated by Formula (5.1): S = K_t √A. Where: S is safety distance, mm; K_t is approach speed parameter, mm/s; A is hazardous area, mm². Used for machinery safety guard design.

variable_definitions.name: Safety Distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum distance to prevent access to hazard

variable_definitions.name: Approach Speed Parameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Parameter for human body approach speed

potential_questions: Given hazard area A=10000 mm², K_t=1600 mm/s, calculate safety distance S.
task_description: 1. Obtain K_t and A; 2. Calculate √A; 3. Calculate S = K_t × √A; 4. Output S.

full_context: According to ISO 13857-2019 "Safety of machinery — Safety distances to prevent hazard zones being reached by upper and lower limbs", Chapter 4, safety distance is calculated by Formula (4.1): S = K(T₁+T₂)+C. Where: S is safety distance, mm; K is body part speed, mm/s; T₁ is machine stop time, s; T₂ is additional response time, s; C is additional distance, mm. Used for positioning safety devices.

variable_definitions.name: Approach Speed
variable_definitions.meaning: Speed of human body part toward hazard

variable_definitions.name: Machine Stop Time
variable_definitions.meaning: Time from stop signal to full halt

potential_questions: Given K=1600 mm/s, T₁=0.1 s, T₂=0.02 s, C=150 mm, calculate safety distance S.
task_description: 1. Obtain K, T₁, T₂, C; 2. Calculate T₁+T₂; 3. Calculate K×(T₁+T₂); 4. Calculate S = K×(T₁+T₂)+C; 5. Output S.

full_context: According to ISO 14119-2013 "Safety of machinery — Interlocking devices associated with guards — Principles for design and selection", Appendix A, dangerous failure rate per hour is calculated by Formula (A.1): PFH_d = λ_DU × DC. Where: PFH_d is dangerous failure frequency per hour, 1/h; λ_DU is dangerous undetected failure rate, 1/h; DC is diagnostic coverage. Used for SIL evaluation.

variable_definitions.name: Dangerous Failure Frequency per Hour
variable_definitions.meaning: Probability of dangerous failure per hour

potential_questions: Given λ_DU=1×10⁻⁶ 1/h, DC=0.9, calculate PFH_d.
task_description: 1. Obtain λ_DU and DC; 2. Calculate PFH_d = λ_DU × DC; 3. Output PFH_d.

full_context: According to ISO 13849-2015 "Safety of machinery — Safety-related parts of control systems", Chapter 4, performance level is approximated by Formula (4.1): PL ≈ 1/PFH_d. Where: PL is mean time to dangerous failure, h; PFH_d is dangerous failure frequency per hour, 1/h. Used for safety control system design.
potential_questions: Given PFH_d=1×10⁻⁶ 1/h, calculate mean time to dangerous failure PL.
task_description: 1. Obtain PFH_d; 2. Calculate PL ≈ 1/PFH_d; 3. Output PL.

full_context: According to ISO 14118-2017 "Safety of machinery — Prevention of unexpected start‑up", Chapter 3, safety stop time is estimated by Formula (3.1): t_s = S/v. Where: t_s is safety stop time, s; S is safety distance, mm; v is human approach speed, mm/s. Used for setting safety control timing.

variable_definitions.name: Safety Stop Time
variable_definitions.meaning: Time to stop hazardous motion completely

variable_definitions.name: Human Approach Speed
variable_definitions.meaning: Speed of body part toward hazard

potential_questions: Given safety distance S=500 mm, approach speed v=1600 mm/s, calculate stop time t_s.
task_description: 1. Obtain S and v; 2. Calculate t_s = S/v; 3. Output t_s.

full_context: According to ISO 14120-2015 "Safety of machinery — Guards — General requirements for the design and construction of fixed and movable guards", Appendix B, force on guard is estimated by Formula (B.1): F = M/e. Where: F is applied force, N; M is applied moment, N·mm; e is lever arm length, mm. Used for guard strength design.

variable_definitions.name: Applied Force
variable_definitions.meaning: Force from human or tool on guard

variable_definitions.name: Lever Arm Length
variable_definitions.meaning: Distance from force application to pivot

potential_questions: Given moment M=5000 N·mm, lever arm e=100 mm, calculate force F.
task_description: 1. Obtain M and e; 2. Calculate F = M/e; 3. Output F.
